pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Hyperammonemic Crisis due to Late-Onset Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) Deficiency THE SMOKING GUN: The critical, time-sensitive lab that was drawn but not available until 10 hours post-intubation was the quantitative ammonia level. Plasma Ammonia Level: 1,120 micromol/L (Reference Range <35 micromol/L). Further metabolic testing revealed markedly elevated urinary orotic acid and low plasma citrulline, confirming OTC Deficiency. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) Deficiency is the most common inherited disorder of the Urea Cycle, usually presenting catastrophically in infancy (X-linked dominant, though males are typically more severely affected). In late-onset presentations, residual enzyme activity is often sufficient to manage nitrogen load until triggered by a significant catabolic stressor, such as a high-protein meal, severe infection, or, as in this case, the stress of the acute illness itself. The deficiency prevents the efficient conversion of ammonia (a byproduct of protein metabolism) into urea. Unmetabolized ammonia rapidly accumulates in the bloodstream (hyperammonemia), easily crossing the blood-brain barrier. In the central nervous system, ammonia is processed by astrocytes, converting glutamate into glutamine. This massive influx of glutamine inside the astrocytes is osmotically active, causing profound astrocyte swelling, leading directly to the observed cytotoxic cerebral edema and subsequent brain herniation, coma, and death if not treated immediately with nitrogen scavengers (e.g., sodium phenylacetate, sodium benzoate) and emergent hemodialysis or CRRT to filter the circulating ammonia.
